words: Neither of us made any money. Because town was amazing after the support of two papers. So you cut prices and you cut prices on ads and you cut prices on printing and to survive. We both survived, but I'm sure neither of us made any money. The logical thing was to merge or have one of them quit and so forth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you thought of why Robinson started the paper in the first place, I mean, in your understanding of why at the time, why do you think he had done it? Just out of spite?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I think out of spite, yes. Partly out of spite and partly out of, oh, conceit that he wanted to be a big shot. He wanted to own a newspaper and inflated ego as a publisher of a newspaper. He could go back all over the country and say, I own a newspaper, I own three drugstores and I do this and I do that as part of the ego image that he wanted to create. And he did run. He had three drugstores here. We got mad at one of them and decided to run him out of business and the best way was to buy a drugstore and open two new ones and he did that. He was a man of peculiar philosophy and he got mad at one of the druggists. I forget which one. So I'll show you, I'll run you out of business. So he had three of them. Which meant that no drugstore in Moscow was profitable. He cut the liver out of all of them and gradually closed one, then he closed another and then he closed the third.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he wasn't really successful in this?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, no.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: He poured a lot of money into them. That's his nature. And I'm satisfied he got mad at George Lanford and he went open the newspaper. Also because it created an image that he wanted to reflect over the nation. As a newspaper owner, as an owner of several drugstores and as a driving a Duesenberg car and all these things, it was part of his ego image.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about this Robinson Lake that you gave to the county?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Well, it was a spot for picnickers and the soil conservation service put in a dam to create a small lake and prevent flooding down below. And so Robinson decided that he would buy the land surrounding that and give it to the county as a gesture of goodwill to win the approval of the people of the area.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So he did.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: And the county commissioners named it Robinson Lake in honor of the donor of the property. He gave him the property. I don't know how much it cost him. Not a great deal. Maybe seven or eight thousand dollars, which was of course worth a lot more than that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But he liked to claim, and I've seen him claim it in more than one place, that he prevented by buying, getting that land, he prevented it from being developed for resort purposes.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Oh, I doubt that. There was no attempt ever made to develop that as a resort. It wasn't big enough. And of course in the very few years it's silted up and it's dry now. That was by then not too long ago and it's dry. It's just silted up and full of weeds because it wasn't deep enough. It was built for a purpose to catch the waters of the creek and of course as a timber was removed from the mountains, the flow in the creek became less and less and so it was no longer a threat. But 40 years ago it was a threat. It used to flood down below.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Let me ask you about social life in Moscow in the 20s.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you came back here and started in the newspaper and in those earlier years what were the main social activities, let's say, that you would be involved in?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Old dances, friends visiting back and forth, that's about it. There were many more dances then there are now. The Elks used to have dances frequently and of course now they have an open dancing every weekend I guess for people to go there. My wife has belonged to one sorority, I belong to a fraternity. We'd be invited to their dances and you'd go to a dance and you'd play cards and go to the movie, that's about it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It wasn't a very structured social life with a lot of organizational affiliations. Not too many, no.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: We had your lodges and they would have dances more so than they have now. I'm sure the Moose and the Eagles, which I did not belong, they used to have dances. They may still, as far as I know, I just don't know whether they do or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the political party activities?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: My understanding has been that Moscow was very strong Republican town. Used to be. At that time. Yes, at that time it was. I would say you're correct. Laitau County was one of the Republican strongholds in those days. Probably the most Republican county in the state of Idaho.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know why?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: No, I don't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's curious to me. I don't know.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I don't know why, it just was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I know the GAR. We were very strong here in the early, early days. It seemed like there were a lot of Northerners that had pretty strong civil war feelings.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: That's about as far as I've gotten an understanding. I think the GAR in the early days, the early 1900s, was strong all over the state of Idaho. Very active in Boise where I was reared. The GAR was quite active. They had their own building and it was quite an active organization. Of course it died when the members died and that was the end of it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you take part in politics at all over here?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, none at all.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I promised my wife I would never run for public office. I think she made me promise that before she'd marry me. She's in the other room, you could ask her. But I took no active part in politics. Never have. Never went to the meetings, never was on the central committee or never ran for office or anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that just because it doesn't interest you or the principal?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I wouldn't want to be in public office.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I wouldn't want to be in politics. I just didn't take any active part at all.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a special reason why she wouldn't want you to?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Well, it used to be in the early days that the first thing a newspaper editor of a small paper would do, would be get in politics. Run for political office. That was common all over the country. So I didn't want to. I think it was better for the paper that I didn't.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Also, we were doing printing for public bodies. And if I had been in the city council, we couldn't have done any printing for the city of Moscow. So, just you'd be hauled into court if you did. A man at Lewiston who had a hardware store was on the state road commission in the state of Idaho. And he sold some equipment to the state highway department. The attorney general immediately raised the question that he could not accept payment for that because he was a member of the state highway commission. So he immediately resigned his state highway commission ship. And so, if you're in politics and doing public printing, you couldn't. Now, as far as the newspaper's concerned, that's different. But commercial printing would be the place where you'd get caught. You couldn't do any for the county if you had a county position. The city if you were on the city council. If you were in the legislature, you couldn't do any state printing. We did a lot of state printing. Always have. University of Idaho state printing. So it'd be caught up in that conflict of interest.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I understand that the newspaper had a cooperative program at the university for getting some training to journalism students. Yes.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: We did for many, many years through the journalism department at the university. There was no formal agreement. The teachers at the university journalism department wanted to know whether I could use some of their senior students as reporters. And I said yes. So they reported to me for like a laboratory. And the lab work would be twice a week to spend three hours at the Idaho only. And I'd send them out to get a story. I'd say go to the high school and cover the high school today or get this story or that story. And it was informal. I mean, nothing ever formally signed. Just informal. And that went on for many years. I don't know if they still do it or not or not. I've seen some articles that the ad hocooning has run in the last few years written by a university student. I just don't know whether. I've seen them employ people after the graduate. Yes. Oh yes. They're doing that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: But I don't know whether they have any informal agreement to use students as reporters or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We did. Give me an idea of what you viewed as broad coverage of the town. What you thought was appropriate to cover that made the news in Moscow.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Anything that somebody else was interested in. If you're interested in this way, then I should run it for you. If you're interested in a report of the Chamber of Commerce, I should run it. So a judgment of whether it's of interest to the community or not.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I can't find news. News is what somebody else wants to read.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or hear.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How big was the staff in the 20s and 30s?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Oh, in the 20s, staff was about three. In the 30s, about four. Then after we merged, my staff was probably for the newspaper, like five. I don't know what it is now. Seven or eight.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Staff in meaning what positions?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Oh, editorial positions. You know, reporters or that nature.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that including yourself?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: No, not including myself. And of course, when you come to the printing department, it was much larger, naturally. But I meant staff, meaning the, uh, the repertorial staff and editorial staff. It was small and gradually grew. I don't know what it is now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me that you had someone stationed in Poland?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: We did have, in about the 1950s, we had an office over there. Right on Main Street. Staffed by one or two people. And covering the news and trying to look out for circulation. The office was on Main Street, a poem. We operated that for several years. It was still being operated when I retired. And I know they closed it not too long after that. And I see that they just announced the other day they had a correspondent from Poland. But I don't think they have an office there. We had an office there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In those days, was there a key business rivalry between Moscow and Poland?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, certainly. Certainly. There still is.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Always has been.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As far as I know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How does it compare with the rivalry in Lewiston?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Oh, more intense. Lewiston's that much farther away, that much harder to get to. There is now a great rivalry between Colfax and Pullman and Spokane. A lot of people shot in Spokane. And of course, Pullman and Moscow being close together, there's always been an interchange of people between the two. People from Moscow go over there to see what the furniture stores got and sometimes buy. And people from Pullman come to Moscow to see what the stores have got here and buy. But we've got a much bigger business area than Pullman has. So there are more come this way than that way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you shed or speculate for me? Shed a little light on why that is so that Moscow seems to be so much more dynamic as a business community than Poland?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Well, it's the fact that we had a more vigorous bunch of merchants. Merchants who were more aggressive and built up their own businesses to attract trade. We had, when I first came here, Mr. Williamson, who had a big department store, which is now the Thutuna Apartments. He was a vigorous merchandiser. We had David's store, which was a vigorous merchandiser under Franklin David and later his sons, who were all in the business. And they were, you know, competitors, promoters. And Pullman just never had any good, solid merchants. They were lackadaisical. They didn't go out and push and compete so that they didn't grow and we did. That's why we got the shopping center west of town and Pullman didn't. It was a cinch two years ago that the first one that started was going to get it. Well, this one started first. The merchants here don't like it, but I think it's going to help the town. My viewpoint is it will help the town. My viewpoint is if I was going to have a restaurant, I'd want to be right next door to the other restaurant. Track the trade. So I think the stores out there will attract shoppers from a wider area. And the merchants in downtown Main Street are going to get the fringe benefits. They can't find what they want out there. They're going to come downtown here and find it. The more traffic you've got, the more merchandise you're going to sell. That's going to be an asset to the community. And I think it will be an asset to every retail merchant in town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that it may initially have a depressing effect on some of the town's people and merchants?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't think so.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: I could be wrong, but I don't think so because, say, any businessman will say he wants to be where his competitor is. As close to him as he can.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But the only question that I have about it, one thing I wonder, is the size of the potential market. If there are going to be enough more people coming in because of what's being offered, you wouldn't come into Moscow to offset the competitive effect of us.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Well, I think it will because people from Moscow now go down to Lewiston to shop at Kmart. They have a Kmart at Lewiston. They go down there to shop down there because they have bigger stores and more stores. They'll drive that 39 miles. Well, now, for the thing out here, it's going to bring people from a wider area. More are going to come to Moscow to shop from Polus than came before because they have Kmart out there. They have a less -dressed drugstore and we've got a drug fair here and it's greater selection, more choice. So they'll come from farther away. I think more people will come from Colfax to Moscow and Polus and Genesee and around. It'll take certain trade from south of here that now goes to Lewiston, nor come to Moscow if it's shorter.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It strikes me that Moscow has a lot more stability as a city does. When you look at the two towns, there's much more continuity there. It seems there's a lot more just altogether turnover.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Well, I think part of that is the fact that Washington State University has a greater impact upon Poland than the University of Idaho has upon Moscow. Washington State College is just double the size of the University of Idaho and I think it overshadows the town to a greater extent than it does the university. So, overshadowing it, they've been more active. The faculty of WSU has been more active in politics, in running the city council and overshadowing the town in the service clubs, the kawanas and rotary and so forth. They dominate to a greater extent. And that hurts the town. And Moscow has a much better balance.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The service community in Moscow is very strong. I think that's correct.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have I helped you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yes. A good deal.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I think the one more thing that I'd like to ask you about is, as you went on in the newspaper working, did you find you got your education on the job to learn to do that?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: No, I, oh, as a senior in high school I was a part time reporter for Boise newspaper. As a senior in high school. I worked for that newspaper, the old Capitol News, during the summer before I came to university. And I liked it and as soon as I got up here why I started working on the Argonaut. And after about a year, we were getting into the recession of the 1920, I realized that I was a burden upon my dad and so I freelanced. And I got to be a correspondent for half a dozen newspapers.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that how you put yourself in the field?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Yes, correct. I didn't realize that. I wrote for the Salt Lake Tribune, the Portland Oregonian, the Seattle Times, the Spokane Chronicle, the Oregon Journal, the Seattle PI, I had both newspapers in Seattle so I'd write two stories. And I wrote for the Christian Science Monitor and as a senior in my last year at university I was averaging $300 a month income and wages were 35 cents an hour. I made more money then than I made after I left here by far. I took the job for $100 a month but I made $300 a month my last year in college.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Freelancing. That's interesting. It sounded like that was a... So I had training.

speaker: Louis Boas
words: And you had a long standing interest. A long standing interest. I think I told you before I went back to Westinghouse Company in the publicity department as a ghost writer. And then got disgusted with Pittsburgh and came out to Boise and got a job on the Idaho State School. And then the chance came to come up here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you know what it was that attracted you to journalism?

speaker: Louis Boas
words: Nope. I have no idea. Just liked it. Better than I liked engineering. That's what you said.
